file_name: Quaker Chemical Corporation - NON COMPETITION AND NON SOLICITATION AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: (d) Each Seller agrees that for a period commencing on the Effective Date and ending three years after the Closing Date (the "Non-Solicit Period"), each Seller shall not, directly or indirectly: (i) induce, solicit, recruit or attempt to persuade any employee of the Combined Business to terminate his or her employment with the Buyer or any of its subsidiaries, or (ii) solicit the employment of any of the employees of the Combined Business. Notwithstanding the above, Sellers shall not be restricted from (1) soliciting for employment or hiring former employees of Buyer or the Company (including their respective subsidiaries) whose employment was terminated by Buyer or the Company (including their respective subsidiaries) at least six months prior to such initial solicitation by such Seller or (2) soliciting employees of the Combined Business by means of a general solicitation through a public medium or general or mass mailing that is not specifically targeted at employees or former 3 employees of the Combined Business; provided, however, that this clause (2) shall not permit any Seller to hire any such employees during the Non-Solicit Period.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) Each Seller agrees that for a period commencing on the Effective Date and ending three years after the Closing Date (the "Non-Solicit Period"), each Seller shall not, directly or indirectly: (i) induce, solicit, recruit or attempt to persuade any employee of the Combined Business to terminate his or her employment with the Buyer or any of its subsidiaries, or (ii) solicit the employment of any of the employees of the Combined Business. Notwithstanding the above, Sellers shall not be restricted from (1) soliciting for employment or hiring former employees of Buyer or the Company (including their respective subsidiaries) whose employment was terminated by Buyer or the Company (including their respective subsidiaries) at least one month prior to such initial solicitation by such Seller or (2) soliciting employees of the Combined Business by means of a general solicitation through a public medium or general or mass mailing that is not specifically targeted at employees or former 3 employees of the Combined Business; provided, however, that this clause (2) shall not permit any Seller to hire any such employees during the Non-Solicit Period.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the waiting period before soliciting former employees from six months to one month could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under Pennsylvania law, potentially making the non-solicitation agreement unenforceable. The reasonableness depends on the specific facts, but a shorter period increases the risk of violating the principle of protecting legitimate business interests without unduly restricting an individual's ability to earn a living.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law on non-compete agreements (unenforceability of unreasonable restraints of trade)
perturbation.law_citation: Pittsburgh Logistics Sys., Inc. v. Beemac Trucking, LLC, 630 Pa. 440, 106 A.3d 764 (2014)
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete agreements by considering factors such as the temporal and geographic scope, and the protection of legitimate business interests. Shortening the waiting period before soliciting former employees to one month may be deemed unreasonable if it unduly restricts a former employee's ability to seek employment and if the employer's legitimate business interests are not sufficiently threatened by such solicitation after only one month.
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the waiting period before soliciting former employees from six months to one month. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this could be an unreasonable restraint of trade under Pennsylvania law, potentially making the non-solicitation agreement unenforceable. The Law Explanation states Pennsylvania courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete agreements by considering factors such as the temporal scope and the protection of legitimate business interests, suggesting the one-month period may be deemed unreasonable if it unduly restricts a former employee's ability to seek employment.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Notices. All notices, requests, consents, claims, demands, waivers and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been given: (a) when delivered by hand (with written confirmation of receipt); (b) when received by the addressee if sent by a nationally recognized overnight courier (receipt requested); (c) on the date sent by facsimile or e-mail of a PDF document (with confirmation of transmission) if sent during normal business hours of the recipient, and on the next Business Day if sent after normal business hours of the recipient or (d) on the third day after the date mailed, by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Notices. All notices, requests, consents, claims, demands, waivers and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been given: (a) when delivered by hand (with written confirmation of receipt); (b) when received by the addressee if sent by a nationally recognized overnight courier (receipt requested); (c) on the date sent by facsimile or e-mail of a PDF document (with confirmation of transmission) if sent during normal business hours of the recipient, and immediately even if sent after normal business hours; or (d) on the tenth day after the date mailed, by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice provisions to make email effective immediately regardless of business hours and delaying mailed notices to ten days could create issues under Pennsylvania contract law, which generally requires reasonable notice provisions. The immediate effectiveness of email, even outside business hours, could be problematic if it prejudices a party's ability to respond promptly. The extended delay for mailed notices may be considered unreasonable, especially for time-sensitive matters.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania contract law regarding reasonableness of notice provisions.
perturbation.law_citation: Empire Props., Inc. v. Equireal, Inc., 449 Pa.Super. 476, 674 A.2d 297 (1996)
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania courts require that contractual notice provisions be reasonable. A notice provision that deems email effective immediately, regardless of whether it's received during business hours, may be deemed unreasonable.  Similarly, a ten-day delay for mailed notices may also be found unreasonable, particularly if it prejudices a party's rights or ability to respond in a timely fashion. Courts consider the totality of the circumstances in determining reasonableness.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that email notices are deemed given on the date sent if during business hours, and the next business day if sent after business hours. The changed text alters this by stating that email notices are effective immediately, even if sent after business hours. Furthermore, the original text states mailed notices are effective on the third day after mailing, while the changed text extends this to ten days. According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, Pennsylvania courts require contractual notice provisions to be reasonable, and these changes may be deemed unreasonable, particularly the immediate effectiveness of email outside business hours and the extended delay for mailed notices. This creates an inconsistency that could prejudice a party's ability to respond in a timely fashion, violating the requirement for reasonable notice. Since no law snippets were provided, the decision is based on the LLM generated explanations and the perturbation explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Injunctive Relief; Attorneys Fees. Each Seller agrees that in the event of a breach of this Agreement, the damage to Buyer will be inestimable and that therefore any remedy at Law or in monetary damages shall be inadequate. Accordingly, the parties agree that Buyer shall, in addition to monetary damages incurred by reason of any such breach or potential breach, without the necessity of posting any bond or similar instrument (and Sellers hereby irrevocably waive any right it may have to require the obtaining, furnishing or posting of any such bond or similar instrument) be entitled to seek injunctive relief (including specific performance) against the Sellers for breach of this Agreement. If any action or proceeding shall be commenced to enforce this Agreement or any right arising in connection with this Agreement, the prevailing party in such action or proceeding shall be entitled to recover from the other party the reasonable attorneys' fees, costs and expenses incurred by such prevailing party in connection with such action or proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Injunctive Relief; Attorneys Fees. Each Seller agrees that in the event of a breach of this Agreement, the damage to Buyer will be inestimable and that therefore any remedy at Law or in monetary damages shall be inadequate. Accordingly, the parties agree that Buyer shall, in addition to monetary damages incurred by reason of any such breach or potential breach, without the necessity of posting any bond or similar instrument (and Sellers hereby irrevocably waive any right it may have to require the obtaining, furnishing or posting of any such bond or similar instrument) be entitled to seek injunctive relief (including specific performance) against the Sellers for breach of this Agreement. If any action or proceeding shall be commenced to enforce this Agreement or any right arising in connection with this Agreement, the prevailing party in such action or proceeding shall be entitled to recover from the other party attorneys' fees but only if the proceeding is settled out of court. The prevailing party cannot recover attorneys fees for a judgment rendered by a judge.
perturbation.explanation: Pennsylvania generally follows the American Rule regarding attorney's fees, which states that parties are responsible for their own legal fees unless there is a specific statutory provision or contractual agreement stating otherwise. Limiting attorneys' fees recovery to only out-of-court settlements contradicts Pennsylvania law where a prevailing party can recover attorney's fees if the agreement allows it regardless if the proceeding resulted in a settlement or judgment by a judge.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania adherence to the American Rule regarding attorney's fees
perturbation.law_citation: Mosaica Academy Charter School v. Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, Department of Education, 645 Pa. 637, 182 A.3d 448 (2018).
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania adheres to the American Rule, which generally prevents a prevailing party from recovering attorneys' fees from the losing party unless an exception applies. This exception can arise through a statute or a contractual agreement. By limiting the recovery of attorney's fees to out-of-court settlements, the modified text contradicts Pennsylvania law which allows for recovery if there is a specific agreement regardless of if the final result was a settlement or judgment.
